masthead.title_html: Reason Your Way Through the Big Cats
masthead.title_plain: Reason Your Way Through the Big Cats
masthead.dek: Seven famous big cats — lion, tiger, leopard, jaguar, cheetah, cougar, and snow leopard — and seven different ways to measure them. You know the whole field; the only question is which cat tops each one. Narrow it down, place your bet, then tap to check.
masthead.footer_html: Seven questions · seven distinct dimensions (100% unique) · a bounded, walkable field · sources noted per question.

plan.note: Bounded-set design: each of the seven big cats is the answer exactly once, on a distinct dimension. The field is fully enumerable, so the player reasons over a known list rather than recalling.

questions.q: Pound for pound, which of the big cats bites down with more force than any other?
questions.detail: About 1,500 PSI — the strongest bite of any cat relative to its body size.
questions.why: Lions and tigers are bigger and bite harder in raw numbers, but for its size the jaguar wins: short jaws give it extra leverage, so it kills by punching straight through the skull — and can even crack the armored hide of a caiman.
questions.more: In absolute force a tiger still bites harder, simply because it is a far larger animal.
questions.source: Catster and Discover Wildlife summaries of felid bite-force studies (jaguar ~1,500 PSI, strongest relative to size).

questions.q: Which big cat makes its home higher up the mountains than any other?
questions.detail: Lives mostly between about 3,000 and 5,400 m; one was tracked at 5,848 m.
questions.why: It is built for the heights — broad paws that work like snowshoes, a thick smoky coat, and an extra-long tail it wraps around itself for warmth and uses for balance on the cliffs.
questions.more: No other big cat lives so high; the rest keep to forests, plains, and foothills far below.
questions.source: Snow Leopard Trust; WWF (typical range 3,000–5,400 m; record 5,848 m).

questions.q: Which big cat's roar is the loudest of all — carrying farther across the land than any other's?
questions.detail: Around 114 decibels up close, and audible up to about 8 km (5 miles) away.
questions.why: Flat, square vocal folds let a lion roar far louder than its size suggests, so a pride can claim territory and call to one another clear across the savanna.
questions.more: Only four cats can truly roar — lion, tiger, leopard, and jaguar — and the lion is the loudest of them.
questions.source: A-Z Animals; Lions Tigers & Bears (lion roar ~114 dB, audible ~8 km).

questions.q: Which big cat roams across more of the world than any other, at home from deserts to rainforests?
questions.detail: Found across roughly 62 countries in Africa and Asia — the widest range of any big cat.
questions.why: Leopards are the great survivors: they eat almost anything and stash their kills up trees, so they thrive from African grassland to Russian snow to Arabian desert — the only big cat that lives in both desert and rainforest.
questions.more: Even so, they have vanished from about two-thirds of their historic range as people take over the land.
questions.source: Panthera; African Wildlife Foundation (leopard range ~62 countries, largest of any big cat).

questions.q: Which big cat can spring higher from a standstill than any other — several times its own height straight up?
questions.detail: Jumps about 5.5 m (18 ft) straight up; the mammal high-jump record is a puma at roughly 7 m.
questions.why: Oversized, muscular hind legs coil and release like springs, letting a cougar bound onto a high ledge or into a tree — sometimes hauling prey heavier than a deer with it.
questions.more: It also leaps up to about 12 m (40 ft) forward in a single bound. It cannot roar, though — so it purrs like a house cat.
questions.source: Guinness World Records (highest jump by a mammal, puma ~7 m); ScienceInsights (~5.5 m vertical).

questions.q: Which big cat is the largest and heaviest of them all?
questions.detail: Siberian tigers reach about 3.7 m in length and up to ~300 kg — the biggest cat on Earth.
questions.why: Tigers hunt big prey alone, which favored sheer bulk, so they out-size every other cat. The lion — the supposed 'king of beasts' — is really the runner-up.
questions.more: A male lion tops out around 230 kg, well short of a large Siberian or Bengal tiger.
questions.source: Britannica; Guinness World Records (largest feline carnivore: tiger, Siberian up to ~300 kg).

questions.q: Which big cat can sprint faster than any other — faster, in fact, than any animal on land?
questions.detail: Hits about 100–110 km/h (60–70 mph) in short bursts.
questions.why: Everything about it is tuned for the sprint — a whip-flexible spine, long legs, and claws that stay out for grip — but it can hold top speed for only 20–30 seconds before it overheats.
questions.more: That speed comes at a cost: a cheetah is too slight to defend its catch, so lions and hyenas often steal it.
questions.source: Smithsonian's National Zoo; National Geographic (cheetah top speed ~100–110 km/h, fastest land animal).
